t: What is 134 - 68?
e: Regroup ones: 4 < 8, so take 1 ten. 14 - 8 = 6 ones. Regroup tens: 2 < 6, so take 1 hundred. 12 - 6 = 6 tens. The answer is 66.

t: Sara had 82 books. She gave away 37. How many books does Sara have left?
e: Regroup: 2 < 7, so take 1 ten. 12 - 7 = 5 ones. 7 - 3 = 4 tens. Sara has 45 books left.

t: __ - 45 = 38. What is the missing number?
e: Add the two known numbers: 38 + 45 = 83. Check: 83 - 45 = 38. The missing number is 83.

t: Which problem needs regrouping? A) 86 - 42  B) 73 - 48  C) 99 - 53  D) 68 - 25
e: Check the ones: A) 6 > 2 (no), B) 3 < 8 (yes!), C) 9 > 3 (no), D) 8 > 5 (no). Only 73 - 48 needs regrouping because the top ones digit is smaller.

t: What is 152 - 87?
e: Regroup ones: 2 < 7, so 12 - 7 = 5 ones. Regroup tens: 4 < 8, so 14 - 8 = 6 tens. The answer is 65.

t: A store has 91 balls. They sell 56 balls. How many are left?
e: Regroup: 1 < 6, so take 1 ten. 11 - 6 = 5 ones. 8 - 5 = 3 tens. The store has 35 balls left.

t: __ - 59 = 34. What is the missing number?
e: Add the two known numbers: 34 + 59 = 93. Check: 93 - 59 = 34. The missing number is 93.

t: There are 80 balloons at a party. 36 pop. Which strategy best solves 80 - 36?
o: Regroup: 0 < 6, so change 1 ten into 10 ones
e: Since the ones digit 0 is less than 6, you need to regroup 1 ten into 10 ones. Then 10 - 6 = 4 ones and 7 - 3 = 4 tens. 80 - 36 = 44.

t: Kayla has 75 stickers. She has 28 more than David. How many stickers does David have?
e: Kayla has MORE, so subtract to find David's amount: 75 - 28 = 47. Regroup: 15 - 8 = 7 ones, 6 - 2 = 4 tens. David has 47 stickers.

t: Look at these: 62 - 38 and 64 - 40. Do they give the same answer?
o: Yes, both equal 24 because adding 2 to each number keeps the difference the same
No, different numbers always give different answers
e: 62 - 38 = 24 and 64 - 40 = 24. Adding the same amount (2) to both numbers keeps the difference the same. This trick makes subtraction easier with friendly numbers!

t: What is 143 - 78?
e: Regroup ones: 3 < 8, so 13 - 8 = 5 ones. Regroup tens: 3 < 7, so 13 - 7 = 6 tens. The answer is 65.

t: A toy box has some cars. After taking out 47, there are 36 left. How many cars were in the box?
e: Work backwards with addition: 36 + 47 = 83. There were 83 cars in the box. Check: 83 - 47 = 36.

t: Which problem does NOT need regrouping?
e: Check ones digits: A) 4 < 9, B) 0 < 3, C) 7 > 2, D) 3 < 8. Only 87 - 52 does not need regrouping because 7 is greater than 2.

t: __ - 36 = 47. What is the missing number?
e: Add: 47 + 36 = 83. Check: 83 - 36 = 47. The missing number is 83.

t: A garden has 93 flowers. Deer eat 45 flowers and wind knocks down 0. How many flowers are standing?
e: 93 - 45 = 48. Regroup: 13 - 5 = 8 ones, 8 - 4 = 4 tens. Subtracting 0 more changes nothing. There are 48 flowers standing.

t: Which of these has the GREATEST difference?
e: Calculate: 81 - 47 = 34, 72 - 35 = 37, 93 - 58 = 35, 64 - 29 = 35. The greatest difference is 37 from 72 - 35.

t: What is the best way to subtract 39 from 75?
e: Subtract 40 (a friendly number) then add 1 back because you subtracted 1 too many. 75 - 40 = 35, then 35 + 1 = 36. This compensation strategy is faster than regrouping.

t: A bus had 64 passengers. At the first stop, 28 get off. How many passengers are on the bus?
e: Regroup: 4 < 8, so 14 - 8 = 6 ones. 5 - 2 = 3 tens. There are 36 passengers on the bus.

t: __ - 67 = 29. What is the missing number?
e: Add: 29 + 67 = 96. Check: 96 - 67 = 29. The missing number is 96.

t: Which pair of problems has the same answer?
e: 72 - 38 = 34 and 74 - 40 = 34. Adding 2 to both numbers gives the same difference. Check others: 83 - 47 = 36 vs 85 - 49 = 36 (also same!). But 74 - 40 is the clearest example of the constant difference strategy.
